HEADLINE FORMULAS
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 21 --

FIVE HELPFUL NOTES
1⃣ A few words about how (not) to use this document. This type of collection is meant to be helpful,
but it is NOT meant to be prescriptive; these are not “rules” so much as brainstorm aids.
The more headlines you write, the less you will need a document like this. You’ll gradually become more
familiar with formulas like these—not only by writing them, but by seeing them everywhere in the
content you already consume on a daily basis. To wit...
2⃣ Steal like an artist. Let’s be clear about something: you shouldn’t ever feel bad about copying ideas
like these because nobody “owns” these formulas. They are impossible to plagiarize because no one
person invented any of them; they’re just observable patterns, occasionally given names and labels.
Thomas Hobbes once wrote: “He who says what has never been said before probably says what will
never be said again.” In other words: most truly original thoughts are lunacy, and the rest is recycled. In
this sense, “stealing” is not just permissible but actually necessary for any kind of creation.
On that note, for transparency’s sake: we’ve ripped off these formulas from numerous sources across the
web. We’ve added some of our own along the way, but we’re not bothering to identify which ones
because (like we said) these don’t belong to anyone and we haven’t invented anything here.
3⃣ How to “read” the formulas. You’ll notice that we’ve written the formulas with brackets, parentheses,
bold text, and light text. Here’s what those mean:
➔ [Bracketed items] are determinate variables, where there’s a blank to fill but we have some sense
of what’s going in it, like a ‘Person’ or a ‘Desired Outcome.’
➔ (Parenthetical items) are optional variables, which you can skip/omit if you prefer.
➔ Bold text denotes the main parts of the formula, whether “required” or optional/parenthetical.
➔ Light text is for indeterminate variables, where there’s a blank to fill but we DON’T have any
particular sense of what would go in it. These items just reflect natural opportunities to vary and
enrich the headline formulas, so you’ll see less of this as you move down the document.
4⃣ Keep these flexibilities in mind while reading the formulas. Aside from the different ways you can
fill in these headline formulas, you should remember:
➔ You can alter or even invert the syntax. In other words, you can rearrange the words and
functional blocks and many of these formulas will still work just as well. This is reflected in the
examples we’ve written for some formulas.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 21 --

➔ There’s some natural overlap between categories here. We’ve placed each formula in the
category with the strongest affinity, but some formulas belong in multiple places.
➔ The headline doesn’t (need to) exist in a vacuum. Sometimes (especially in journalism), the
headline should get straight to the point and stand alone—but this isn’t the rule. You can take less
direct, more attention-getting approaches to writing headlines wherever those headlines are
“bumpered” by subheadings and/or the formatted body text which catches a skimmer’s eye.
5⃣ General editing advice: pay close attention to your diction (word choice). The importance of
diction is magnified by (A) the importance of your headline and (B) the natural brevity of headlines. When
you only get a few words, it’s critical to choose good words!
Let’s say you’ve decided how you want to approach your headline (i.e. which formula) and then you just
have to edit it down to final form. Some advice for improving your diction, sorted by parts of speech:
➔ Nouns: Be more specific. Nouns are “things” like ‘cinder block’ (concrete) and ‘idea’ (abstract). The
main problem we see is that people pick bland, generic nouns like ‘people’ or ‘thing’ which don’t
give readers a specific idea of what’s being discussed. Noun specificity is important both (A) for
identifying a key audience like ‘marketers’ or ‘ad buyers’ and (B) for identifying the specific topic at
hand, like ‘mistakes’ or ‘solutions’ or ‘tools’ or ‘steps’.
➔ Verbs: Be more specific and/or more colorful. Verbs are “actions” like ‘click’ (concrete) and ‘think’
(abstract). A lot of writers argue that verbs are the most important part of speech, since they
describe what’s actually happening—which is what you need to make sure you’re doing.
Sometimes, the right verb is unavoidably bland and you’ll need to make the headline interesting
somewhere else; other times, you can pick a more colorful version (‘blow up’ instead of ‘increase’).
➔ Adjectives and Adverbs: If you can’t get rid of them, at least make them interesting.
Adjectives describe things/nouns (e.g. ‘tall’ person) and adverbs either describe actions (e.g.
‘quickly’ grew) or further describe adjectives (e.g. ‘very’ tall person). The trouble with these parts of
speech is that they’re often crutches for weak word choice; to borrow the Dead Poets example, the
phrase “very tired” is a lazy version of exhausted. So when you’re examining adjectives and
adverbs, try first to (1) eliminate them by choosing stronger nouns/verbs and, failing that, try to (2)
choose colorful adjectives and adverbs which grab attention, even if they’re unusual.
➔ Prepositions: You have a whole palette of options here. Prepositions connect the elements of
a sentence; the mnemonic for identifying prepositions is “anywhere a mouse can go.” (Can a
mouse go for something? Yes, “for” is a preposition. Can a mouse go around something? Yes,
“around” is a preposition. Can a mouse go but something? Nope, “but” is a conjunction.) Whenever
you see a preposition, remember that you can usually swap it for a different one; this will help you
smooth out some awkward phrasing during your edits!
www.getwsodo.com
